content.problemStatement:
**Hey Coders!** Welcome to the **Tech Blog Platform Project** 📝💻

In this project, you will build the **Spring Boot Backend** for a content publishing engine. This system acts as the backbone for a blogging site where creators can publish articles and readers can engage with them.

A blog isn't just a list of words; it's a web of relationships. **Authors** write **Posts**, and **Posts** generate **Comments**. Your system will use JPA Mappings to chain these entities together seamlessly.

Your **task** is to **complete the missing backend logic** to make this API fully functional.

---

**Project Structure** \
You are given **8 Java files** representing a **Layered Architecture**:
  - **Entities (The Data Model):**
    - `Author.java` → The **Root** entity. Represents the content creator.
    - `BlogPost.java` → The **Middle** entity. Represents an article written by an Author.
    - `Comment.java` → The **Leaf** entity. Represents reader feedback on a specific Post.
  - **Repositories (The Data Access Layer):**
    - `AuthorRepository`, `BlogPostRepository` & `CommentRepository` → Interfaces that handle saving and retrieving data for their respective entities.
  - **Service (The Business Logic):**
    - `BlogService.java` → Contains the logic to **link** posts to authors, comments to posts, and handle cascading deletions.
  - **Controller (The API Layer):**
    - `BlogController.java` → Handles web requests to publish posts, add comments, and fetch the feed.

**Project Dependencies** \
To build this backend, your project is pre-configured with the following tools:
- **Spring Web:** For building RESTful API endpoints and handling HTTP requests.
- **Spring Data JPA:** For interacting with the database using Java objects instead of SQL.
- **H2 Database:** An in-memory database that stores your data while the app is running.
- **Lombok:** A library that automatically generates boilerplate code like Getters, Setters, and Constructors.

---

**Important Rules**
- Do **NOT** change class names
- Do **NOT** change method names
- Complete the sections marked with **TODO**
- The testing system relies on the existing structure

**The Logic: ORM Relationships** \
This project focuses on **Chained Relationships** and **Performance Tuning**.
1. **The Hierarchy:** Author ➔ BlogPost ➔ Comment.
2. **Cascading:**
  - If you delete an **Author**, the system must delete all their **Posts**.
  - If you delete a **Post**, the system must delete all its **Comments**.
3. **Fetch Types (Performance):**
  - **LAZY:** When loading an Author, don't load their 500 posts immediately (Too heavy!).
  - **EAGER:** When loading a Comment, do load the Post info immediately (We need context!).

---

**Database Access (H2 Console)** \
If you want to visually check your tables and run SQL queries manually:
1. Run the file named `consoleViewer.js` and paste the CodeChef browser URL. For more details, refer to the project demonstration video.
  - Command to run the file:
    ```default
    node consoleViewer.js
    ```
2. Copy the URL printed in the output.
3. Paste it into your browser to access the H2 Console.
4. Write this JDBC URL `"jdbc:h2:mem:techblogdb"` and click **Connect** to see your live data tables (for more details, check `application.properties` file).

---

**Your Task:**
1. **Complete the Entities**
- **File:** `entity/Author.java` (The Root)
  - **Annotations:** Mark this class as a JPA Entity.
  - **Primary Key:** Configure the `id` field to be the Primary Key and set it to **Auto-Increment** using the `IDENTITY` strategy.
  - **Relationship:** Define a list of `blogPosts` using **One-to-Many**.
    - **Challenge 1:** Map it to the `author` field in the `BlogPost` class.
    - **Challenge 2:** Configure `cascade` to **ALL** (Delete Author ➔ Delete Posts).
    - **Challenge 2:** Set `fetch` to **LAZY** (Performance optimization).
  - **JSON Handling:** Add the annotation to **ignore** this list during JSON conversion (Stop Infinite Loops).
  - **Getters & Setters:** Generate these for all fields (manually or using Lombok annotations) so other classes can access the data.
- **File:** `entity/BlogPost.java` (The Connector)
  - **Annotations:** Mark this class so that JPA recognizes it as a database table.
  - **Primary Key:** Configure the `id` field as the Primary Key with Auto-Increment.
  - **Content Field:** The default storage is too small (255 chars).
    - **Challenge:** Add an annotation to set the column length to `5000` characters.
  - **Relationship 1 (Link to Author):**
    - Many Posts belong to One Author.
    - **Fetch:** Set to **EAGER** (Load author name with post).
    - **Foreign Key:** Name the column `"author_id"`.
  - **Relationship 2 (Link to Comments):**
    - One Post has Many Comments.
    - **MappedBy:** Point to the `blogPost` field in Comment.
    - **Cascade:** Set to **REMOVE** (Delete Post ➔ Delete Comments).
    - **Fetch:** Set to **LAZY**.
  - **Getters & Setters:** Generate these for all fields (manually or using Lombok annotations) so other classes can access the data.
- **File:** `entity/Comment.java` (The Leaf)
  - **Annotations:** Mark this class so that JPA recognizes it as a database table.
  - **Primary Key:** Configure the `id` field as the Primary Key with Auto-Increment.
  - **Relationship (Link to Post):**
    - Many Comments belong to One Post.
    - **Fetch:** Set to **EAGER**.
    - **Foreign Key:** Name the column `"blogpost_id"`.
    - **JSON Handling:** Add annotation to **ignore** the `blogPost` field (Stop Infinite Loops).
  - **Getters & Setters:** Generate these for all fields (manually or using Lombok annotations) so other classes can access the data. \
**Docs:** Guide to JPA Entities | Lombok @Getter & @Setter | @Id and @GeneratedValue | Guide to @OneToMany and @ManyToOne | 
Understanding the mappedBy Attribute | JPA Cascade Types | Using @JoinColumn for Foreign Keys | Handling Infinite Recursion with @JsonIgnore

2. **Configure the Repositories** (Code Provided) \
  **File:** `AuthorRepository`, `BlogPostRepository`, `CommentRepository`
  - **Interface:** Define these as Interfaces (not classes).
  - **Inheritance:** Extend the standard `JpaRepository` interface.
  - **Generics:** Specify the Entity Type (e.g., `Author`) and ID Type (e.g., `Long`).

3. **Build the Service** \
  **File:** `service/BlogService.java`
  - **Annotation:** Add the correct Spring annotation to mark this class as a Service.
  - **Dependency Injection:** Define the 3 repositories as `private final` fields and initialize them via **Constructor Injection**.
  - **Logic - Create Post:**
    - Find the `Author` by ID (use `.orElse(null)` logic).
    - If found, **LINK** the Post to the Author (Foreign Key).
    - Save the Post.
    - If not found, throw RuntimeException.
  - **Logic - Add Comment:**
    - Find the `BlogPost` by ID.
    - If found, **LINK** the Comment to the Post.
    - Save the Comment.
  - **Logic - Delete Post:**
    - Delete by ID (Cascade settings in Entity will handle the rest).
  - **Logic - Get All Posts:**
    - Return the full list using the repository. \
**Docs:** @Service Annotation | Constructor Dependency Injection | Spring Data CrudRepository Methods (save, findById)

4. **Define the API Endpoints** \
  **File:** `controller/BlogController.java`
    - **Class Configuration:** Annotate the class to make it a **REST Controller** and define the base URL path as `"/api"`.
    - **Dependency Injection:** Inject the `BlogService` using the constructor.
    - **Endpoints:**
      1. **Create Author** (`POST /authors`)
        - Convert the JSON body to an Author object.
        - **Action:** Call the service method to save the author and return the saved object.
      2. **Create Post** (`POST /authors/{authorId}/posts`)
        - Capture `authorId` from the URL path.
        - Capture `post` data from the Request Body.
        - **Action:** Call the service method that links the post to the author.
      3. **Add Comment** (`POST /posts/{postId}/comments`)
        - Capture `postId` from the URL path.
        - Capture `comment` data from the Request Body.
        - **Action:** Call the service method to add the comment.
      4. **Get Feed** (`GET /posts`)
        - **Action:** Call the service method to get all posts and return the list.
      5. **Delete Post** (`DELETE /posts/{postId}`)
        - Capture `postId` from the URL path.
        - **Action:** Call the service to delete the post (and its comments via Cascade). \
**Docs:** @RestController Annotation | Building a RESTful Web Service | Overview of @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @DeleteMapping | Spring @PathVariable | @RequestBody Annotation

---

**Expected Behaviour:** \
Before submitting the project, ensure the **backend server is running**. If you make any changes to the code, you must **restart the server** for those changes to take effect.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
